did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), to classify skin lesions. Five pre-trained models (ResNet50V2, DenseNet121, InceptionV3, Xception, and MobileNet) were trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 dermoscopic image dataset to classify seven types of skin cancer/lesions. The Xception model achieved the highest accuracy (92.5-92.95%) and was implemented in an Android teledermatology application.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper describes original experimental work involving the development of an Android teledermatology application. It details specific methods including data cleaning, augmentation, and pre-processing of the HAM10000 dataset, training and hyperparameter tuning of five pre-trained CNN models (ResNet50V2, DenseNet121, InceptionV3, Xception, MobileNet), and implementation/evaluation of the best model (Xception) on an Android platform. The results section reports specific accuracy metrics (e.g., 92.95% for Xception) and performance comparisons, which are characteristic of original research rather than a literature review.

title: Android Teledermatology Application for Skin Lesions Classification Based on Dermoscopic Images
journal: Journal of Telecommunication Network (Jurnal Jaringan Telekomunikasi)
brief_summary: This study developed an Android-based teledermatology application to classify seven types of skin lesions using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. Five pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network models (ResNet50V2, DenseNet121, InceptionV3, Xception, and MobileNet) were trained and evaluated with hyperparameter tuning to optimize performance. The Xception model achieved the highest accuracy of 92.95% and was selected for implementation in the mobile application. Testing on the Android platform showed that the application maintained high accuracy for gallery images but experienced a significant performance drop for camera-captured images due to environmental noise and resolution differences. The results highlight the challenges of deploying deep learning models in real-world clinical settings where image quality varies.

lesion_types_studied.details: Largest class in the HAM10000 dataset, comprising around 67% of the total data (5,115 images after cleaning). Augmented to 5,115 images for training.
Second largest class with 1,030 images. Consistently showed lower F1 scores across all models due to high variability in shapes, colors, and textures. Augmented to 4,970 images.
Comprising 940 images. Achieved higher F1 scores than melanoma despite fewer images, suggesting simpler visual patterns. Augmented to 5,000 images.
Comprising 451 images. Augmented to 4,560 images. Listed as one of the classes where augmentation was highly effective.
Comprising 273 images. Augmented to 4,618 images. Listed as one of the classes where augmentation was highly effective.
Smallest class with 119 images. Augmented to 3,027 images. Despite small original size, achieved relatively higher F1 scores than melanoma or benign keratoses due to simpler image features.
Smallest class with 101 images (1% of total data). Augmented to 3,433 images. Achieved relatively higher F1 scores than melanoma or benign keratoses due to simpler image features.

all_task_types.target: Seven types of skin lesions (Melanocytic Nevi, Melanoma, Benign Keratoses, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratoses, Vascular Lesions, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. Five pre-trained CNN models (ResNet50V2, DenseNet121, InceptionV3, Xception, MobileNet) were trained to predict discrete diagnostic categories. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and F1 scores for each class. The Xception model achieved the highest accuracy (92.95%) and was implemented in an Android application for classifying input images into these seven categories.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The original dataset contained 10,015 images. In this study, 4,501 duplicate images were removed, resulting in 5,514 clean images. The images were resized to 224x224 pixels and normalized. Data augmentation was applied to the training set to balance class distribution.

use.training: Used for training five pre-trained CNN models (ResNet50V2, DenseNet121, InceptionV3, Xception, MobileNet) after data cleaning and augmentation. The training set was augmented to 31,123 images to balance class distribution.
use.validation: Used to evaluate model performance during training and for hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, optimizer, batch size, dropout).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final model performance (accuracy and F1 score) after training. Also used to test the Android application's accuracy with gallery images.
use.other: The test set images were also used to evaluate the Android application's performance with camera-captured images, revealing a performance drop due to environmental noise and resolution differences.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To mitigate the impact of anomalous or outlier test results and obtain a more accurate, reliable, and consistent estimate of model performance. The averages were derived from the three best-performing tests out of five for each hyperparameter test.
To divide the dataset into training, validation, and test sets to evaluate model performance during training and final unbiased assessment.
To quantify and compare the classification performance of the five pre-trained CNN models across different hyperparameter settings and lesion classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy and F1-scores, with results averaged over multiple tests to mitigate outlier influence, but no statistical significance testing was performed.

split_70_15_15.notes: Split ratio 70:15:15 yielded an average accuracy of 91.673% and F1-score of 0.753 across models.

split_80_5_15.notes: Split ratio 80:5:15 yielded an average accuracy of 92.237% and F1-score of 0.773 across models.

split_80_10_10.notes: Split ratio 80:10:10 was selected as optimal, yielding an average accuracy of 92.95% and F1-score of 0.803 across models.

Xception.notes: Highest performance among all models. Achieved with batch size 64, dropout 0.5, Adam optimizer, and learning rate from previous best tests.

MobileNet V2.notes: Lowest average performance. Best at batch size 16 and dropout 0.4.

ResNet50 V2.notes: Performed best at batch size 32. Dropout 0.5 yielded F1 0.753.

DenseNet121.notes: Performed best at batch size 32. Dropout 0.5 yielded F1 0.797. Benefited from two-stage training.

Inception V3.notes: Performed best at batch size 64. Dropout 0.5 yielded F1 0.757.

hyperparameter_tuning_results.notes: Results are averages of the three best-performing tests out of five for each hyperparameter test to mitigate outliers.

android_implementation_results.notes: Gallery image accuracy was consistent with Google Colab results. Camera-captured images showed decreased accuracy, particularly for 'nv' (23% drop) and 'df' classes, due to lighting, angle, resolution, and screen artifacts.

architecture_used.main_model: Five pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): ResNet50V2, DenseNet121, InceptionV3, Xception, and MobileNet V2. Xception was selected as the best-performing model.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset. The 'include_top' section was set to 'false' to remove the existing fully connected layers used for predicting 1000 ImageNet classes, allowing customization for seven skin lesion classes.

architecture_details.backbone_modifications: Base layers were modified by removing the top classification layer. Additional layers were added in the following order: Global Average Pooling 2D, Batch Normalization, Dropout, and a Dense layer with softmax activation for multiclass classification.
architecture_details.training_strategy: Models were tested with single-step (fine-tuning all layers) and two-step training (transfer learning first, then fine-tuning). DenseNet121 and Xception benefited from two-step training.

training_details.optimizer: Adam (selected as best performing for Xception and others based on testing)
training_details.loss_function: Categorical Crossentropy (implied by softmax activation and multiclass classification, though not explicitly named in text)

hyperparameters.epochs: 10 to 15 (range tested), stopped early if no validation loss decrease for 3 consecutive epochs

training_details.training_schedule: Early stopping was used to prevent overfitting. Checkpoints were saved at highest accuracy. Adaptive learning rate was reduced if no improvement in validation loss for two epochs.

classification_or_output_head.details: The original ImageNet top was removed. A new dense layer with softmax activation was added for classifying seven skin lesion types.

preprocessing_techniques: Data cleaning: Removal of 4,501 duplicate images from the HAM10000 dataset to produce 5,514 clean images.
Resizing: Images resized from original 650x450 pixels to 224x224 pixels across all RGB layers.
Normalization: Images normalized into specific values based on the pre-trained model's format.

Data Augmentation: Applied to the training set to balance class distribution, including rotation (up to 180 degrees), horizontal and vertical shifting (up to 10%), image scaling (up to 10% enlargement), horizontal and vertical flipping, and 'nearest' approach for filling gaps.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated up to 180 degrees.
Images were shifted horizontally and vertically by up to 10% of the image width or height.
Images were scaled with up to 10% enlargement.
Images were flipped horizontally and vertically.
The 'nearest' approach was used for filling image gaps during operations like rotation or shifting.

Augmentation was applied to minority classes to balance the dataset, targeting approximately 5,000 images per class. Specific post-augmentation counts: Melanocytic Nevi (nv) 5,115; Melanoma (mel) 4,970; Benign Keratoses (bkl) 5,000; Basal Cell Carcinoma (bcc) 4,560; Actinic Keratoses (akiec) 4,618; Vascular Lesions (vasc) 3,027; Dermatofibroma (df) 3,433. Total training images increased from 8,029 to 31,123.

conclusions: The Xception model achieved the highest classification performance among the five tested pre-trained CNNs (ResNet50V2, DenseNet121, InceptionV3, Xception, and MobileNet V2), with an accuracy of 92.95% and an F1 score of 0.803, making it the optimal choice for implementation.
MobileNet V2 demonstrated the lowest average performance with an accuracy of 90.1% and an F1 score of 0.703, despite being computationally lighter.

Two-stage training (transfer learning followed by fine-tuning) significantly improved the performance of complex models like DenseNet121 and Xception, whereas it was not universally beneficial for all architectures.

Data imbalance and the complexity of specific lesion classes significantly impacted model performance, particularly for minority classes like Melanoma ('mel'), which consistently received the lowest F1 scores.
While the Android application maintained high accuracy for gallery images (consistent with offline testing results), there was a noticeable drop in accuracy for camera-captured images.

The 'nv' (Melanocytic Nevi) class experienced the most significant accuracy decrease (up to 23%) when using camera-captured images, likely due to environmental noise, lighting variations, angle differences, and screen artifacts.
The study highlights the challenges of deploying deep learning models in real-world clinical settings where image quality and resolution vary compared to controlled datasets.
Adding more image data from other sources is recommended to reduce data imbalances and improve overall accuracy for minority classes.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., Melanocytic Nevi at ~67% vs. Dermatofibroma at ~1%) and multi-source bias, limiting the generalizability of the findings to other populations or imaging devices.

Data augmentation was used to artificially balance classes, increasing the training set from 8,029 to 31,123 images; this may lead to overfitting on augmented patterns rather than true biological features, particularly for minority classes like Melanoma and Dermatofibroma.

The Xception model achieved high accuracy (92.95%) on the test set, but the F1-score (0.803) indicates significant disparity in performance across classes, with Melanoma consistently showing the lowest F1 scores due to high visual variability.

There is a notable discrepancy between offline model performance and real-world deployment: accuracy dropped significantly (up to 23% for the 'nv' class) when using camera-captured images compared to gallery images, highlighting sensitivity to environmental noise, lighting, and resolution.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values or confidence intervals); performance metrics are averaged over the three best tests out of five to mitigate outliers, which is a robustness measure but not a substitute for rigorous statistical validation.

The Android application was tested on a limited set of images to evaluate deployment performance, lacking a large-scale external validation cohort to assess clinical utility in diverse real-world settings.

The study acknowledges that the 'mel' (Melanoma) class is difficult to classify due to irregular shapes and textures, suggesting that the current model may have high false-negative rates for this critical class in practical applications.
